coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from carbohydrate-rich residue of birch obtained following lignin-first strategy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Ziyu
Hu, Jun
Lu, Ping
Wang, Yunjun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized from carbohydrate-rich residue of birch obtained following the lignin-first strategy. The optical and physicochemical properties of the CQDs were studied, along with their potential for photocatalytic pollutant degradation. By combining solvothermal and chemical oxidation methods, the product yield of CQDs from carbohydrate-rich residue reached 8.1 wt%. Doping nitrogen enhances the graphitization of CQDs and introduces abundant amino groups to the surface, thereby boosted the quantum yield significantly from 8.9 % to 18.7 %-19.3 %. Nitrogen-doped CQDs exhibited efficient photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue, reaching 37 % within 60 min, with a kinetic degradation rate of 0.00725 min−1. This study demonstrates that carbohydrate-rich residue obtained from lignin-first strategy are ideal precursors for synthesizing CQD with high mass yield and quantum yield by combining solvothermal treatment and chemical oxidation methods, offering a novel approach for the utilization of whole biomass components following the lignin-first strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Biomass
Nitrogen-doping
Photocatalysis
Pollutant degradation

Physicochemical characteristics of carbon quantum dots
Synthesis path of carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic degradation of pollutants over carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ultraviolet–visible absorption, fluorescence spectra, and macroscopic feature of three carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (a) CQDs-1, (b) CQDs-2, (c) CQDs-3.

Transmission electron microscope (a, b, c) and particle size statistics (d) of three carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (a) CQDs-1, (b) CQDs-2, (c) CQDs-3, (inset) high-resolution transmission electron microscope.

Concentration ratio (a) and kinetic reaction rate (b) of methylene blue degraded by photocatalysis of different carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Three analyses were conducted. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Concentration ratio (a) and kinetic reaction rate (b) of Rhodamine B degraded by photocatalysis of different carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Three analyses were conducted.
Results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy quantitative analysis of three carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots from carbohydrate-rich residue of birch obtained following lignin-first strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Mechanical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Mechanical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully synthesized from carbohydrate-rich residue of birch obtained following the lignin-first strategy. The optical and physicochemical properties of the CQDs were studied, along with their potential for photocatalytic pollutant degradation. By combining solvothermal and chemical oxidation methods, the product yield of CQDs from carbohydrate-rich residue reached 8.1 wt%. Doping nitrogen enhances the graphitization of CQDs and introduces abundant amino groups to the surface, thereby boosted the quantum yield significantly from 8.9 % to 18.7 %-19.3 %. Nitrogen-doped CQDs exhibited efficient photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue, reaching 37 % within 60 min, with a kinetic degradation rate of 0.00725 min−1. This study demonstrates that carbohydrate-rich residue obtained from lignin-first strategy are ideal precursors for synthesizing CQD with high mass yield and quantum yield by combining solvothermal treatment and chemical oxidation methods, offering a novel approach for the utilization of whole biomass components following the lignin-first strategy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional nanoparticles comprised of carbon cores with a sp2/sp3 hybrid structure, along with various surface functional groups (Ðorđević et al., 2022). The exceptional hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and controllable fluorescence properties of CQDs have garnered significant interest, particularly for applications in metal ion detection, bioimaging, sensing, solar cells, supercapacitor, and photocatalysis (Magagula et al., 2022). Many preparation methods for CQDs have been developed, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, combustion, microwave, and ultrasonic treatment. Among these techniques, solvothermal treatment and chemical oxidation stand out as widely utilized methods due to their facile synthesis process flow and minimal equipment requirements (Li et al., 2021).

CQDs can be synthesized from various carbon-based materials. Biomass, as an abundant renewable resource, has emerged as a promising precursor for CQDs due to the increasing consumption of fossil resources and associated environmental concerns (Cai et al., 2022). Particularly, lignocellulosic biomass, composed of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose as its primary components, is receiving growing attention due to its abundance and low costs (Zhao et al., 2022). Si et al. (2018) prepared lignin nanoparticles and CQDs using the natural lignocellulosic rice straw as the precursor, demonstrating remarkable upconversion fluorescence in the prepared CQDs. Qiu et al. (2022) synthesized CQDs from six kinds of lignocellulosic biomasses, finding that agricultural and forestry biomass with higher nonstructural sugar content can improve the product yield of CQDs. Wang et al. (2020) prepared CQDs using lignocellulosic biomass such as ginkgo biloba leaves and paulownia leaves as carbon sources, observing the significant fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ ions on CQDs. These studies underscore the potential of lignocellulosic biomass as a promising material for synthesizing CQDs with high fluorescence emissivity and good stability (Wareing et al., 2021). Despite these advancements, CQDs synthesized from lignocellulosic biomass still face challenges such as low product yield, relatively low quantum yield, and poor purity. These issues could be attributed in part to the complex three-dimensional structure of lignocellulosic biomass, which exhibits high robustness and diversity.

CQDs derived from lignocellulosic biomass are proposed to be formed through an initial degradation of the polymer into small molecules, followed by the transformation of hydrolyzed products through dehydration, condensation, polymerization forming condensates. With prolonged reaction times, these condensates contract and interconnect to form CQDs (Zhao et al., 2020). Recently, Wu et al. (2021) reported that the properties of CQDs are influenced by the three-component composition of lignocellulosic biomass. Hemicellulose facilitates the formation of CQDs in hydrothermal processes, while lignin exhibits unfavorable effects. Li et al. (2024) and Emanuele et al. (2019) also observed that the different compositions of lignocellulosic precursors have a certain effect on CQDs preparation. Through the lignin-first strategy, native lignin is liberated from the biomass matrix and directly converted, while cellulose and hemicellulose remain as solids. A challenge in the lignin-first strategy is the recovery of heterogeneous catalysts from the solid mixture containing carbohydrates. Jia et al. (2023) have demonstrated that using Ru/C and H2WO4 as co-catalysts efficiently depolymerized native lignin in biomass, yielding monophenols of 40.4 wt% at 200 ℃. By introducing H2WO4 as the acid catalyst, cellulose and hemicellulose retained in the solid residue can be fully hydrolyzed in isopropanol/water at 230 ℃, facilitating facile catalyst recovery. The hydrolysis products of cellulose and hemicellulose are complex, including furans, short-chain alcohols, and acids. Inspired by previous studies, the hydrolysates of cellulose and hemicellulose hold promise as materials for CQDs preparation, and relevant research has not been reported yet.

In this study, hydrolysates of carbohydrate-rich residue obtained in depolymerization of native birch lignin following the lignin-first strategy, were used as the precursor materials for synthesizing CQDs (Jia et al., 2023). Furthermore, a combination of solvothermal method (bottom-up) and chemical oxidation method (top-down) was employed for synthesizing CQDs to enhance the product yield. The optical and physicochemical properties of the synthesized CQDs were investigated, and the potential application in the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants was discussed. This study presents a novel approach for producing high-quality CQDs from lignocellulosic biomass.

Birch was provided by a local manufacturer in Liaoning Province, China, and was milled, sieved to 80–200 mesh, and oven-dried at 105 ℃ overnight before use. Ru/C, H2WO4, CO(NH2)2, Na2SO4, Rhodamine B (RhB), and dialysis bags (500 Da) were purchased from Aladdin and used as received. Isopropyl alcohol, methylene blue (MB), H2SO4 (95 wt%), HCl, and NaOH were purchased from Sinopharm and used without further purification.

Via the lignin-first strategy, native lignin in birch was primarily depolymerized over the heterogeneous metal catalyst of Ru/C cooperated with the temperature-controlled phase-transfer acid of H2WO4, as detailed in previous reports (Jia et al., 2023). Briefly, 2 g of birch, 0.1 g of Ru/C, 0.125 g of H2WO4, and 30 mL of isopropyl alcohol, together with 10 μL of n-decane as the internal standard, were loaded into a 50 mL autoclave (SLM 50, Beijing Century Senlong Experimental Apparatus Co., Ltd), and pressurized with 1 MPa of H2. After depolymerization at 200 ℃ for 4 h while stirring at 600 rpm, the reactor was cooled to room temperature to obtain the product mixture. Then, the product mixture was filtered to obtain the liquid product rich in monophenols and 1.55 g of solid mixture mainly comprising 1.325 g of carbohydrates (cellulose and hemicellulose) along with 0.225 g of catalysts were obtained.

Subsequently, 1.55 g of solid mixture and 30 mL of isopropyl alcohol/water (10 mL/ 20 mL) were reintroduced into the autoclave (SLM 50, Beijing Century Senlong Experimental Apparatus Co., Ltd), followed by pressurization with 1 MPa of H2. The reactor was then heated to 230 °C, and held at this temperature for 8 h while stirring at 600 rpm. After this treatment, the retained carbohydrates were fully hydrolyzed, and 0.225 g of solid catalysts were readily recovered after filtration for reuse. The hydrolysate contains over 90 compounds, including 26.7 % alcohols, 28.3 % ketones, 0.4 % aldehydes, 6.6 % acids, 15.7 % ethers, 7.8 % esters, 3.7 % furans, and 9.5 % phenols (in peak area ratio) (see supplementary material). The formation of a large number of short-chain alcohols and ketones may be attributed to the high hydrolysis temperature (230 °C) and the presence of Ru/C and H2. The hydrolysate is then used as the precursor for synthesizing CQDs in the following procedures.

Initially, 30 mL of hydrolysate was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, sonicated for 20 min (the ultrasonic power is set as 480 W and the ultrasonic frequency is 40 kHz), and then kept at 180 °C for 8 h. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to yield supernatant A. Subsequently, supernatant A was purified in dialysis bags (MWCO of 500) for 72 h, with deionized water outside the bags being replaced every 8 h. Following dialysis, the solution underwent another round of centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the resulting supernatant B was filtered through 0.22 μm membranes and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain CQDs-1.

The synthesis process for CQDs-2 closely resembled that of CQDs-1, with the exception of the addition of 1.5 g of urea to the 30 mL of hydrolysate prior to hydrothermal processing, aimed at synthesizing the nitrogen-doped CQDs.

During the dialysis stage of CQDs-2 preparation, a substantial amount of solid carbon product emerged. This solid carbon product was collected after the 8000 rpm centrifugation to obtain supernatant B. The solid was rinsed with deionized water, dried overnight at 60 °C, and subsequently used for synthesizing CQDs. Specifically, 0.1 g of the dried solid was mixed with 50 mL of NaOH solution (0.2 mol/L), ultrasonicated for 20 min at room temperature, followed by the addition of 4 mL of H2O2. The mixture was then stirred for 8 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the product mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. After adjusting the pH to 6–7 with HCl, the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm membranes, purified in dialysis bags for 72 h, and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain CQDs-3.

Because solid carbohydrate-rich residue serves as the starting precursor material and its mass is easily determined by the difference between the solid mixture and the solid catalysts, the mass yields of CQDs were calculated based on the mass of solid carbohydrate-rich residue (Chai et al., 2019; Jing et al., 2019).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were captured using a JEM-2100 Plus transmission electron microscope from JEOL. Raman spectra were recorded using a HORIBA LabRAM HR Evolution Epectrometer. FTIR spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Frontier FT-IR Spectrometer using KBr pellets containing approximately 1 % of the samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Bruker D8 Advance instrument using Cu Kα radiation (60 kV, 80 mA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250xi instrument employing Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV).

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a Cary 5000 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence performance of CQDs was determined using a LS-50B fluorescence spectrophotometer from Perkin-Elmer. The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was determined using quinine sulfate as the standard reference (Qys = 54 %). The calculation formula is as follows (Wu et al., 2017),
(1)
Q Y
C
=
QY
S
A s
A C
I C
I s
η
C
2
η
s
2

Where the corner label C denotes CQDs, while S represents quinine sulfate. QY (%) is quantum yield, A is the absorbance at the same excitation wavelength which was maintained within the range of 0.05 to 0.1, I is the fluorescence integral intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, with ηC=ηS=1.33. The quinine sulfate solution was diluted with 0.1 mol/L of H2SO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of dyes over carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the performance of CQDs in photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, 5 mg of powdered CQDs was dispersed in 100 mL of MB solution (10 mg/L). The mixture was magnetically stirred in darkness for 30 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Subsequently, photocatalytic degradation was initiated under the irradiation of a 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-PF300-T6) equipped with a 420 nm cut-off filter. A circulating water cooler was employed to maintain the reaction temperature at 20 °C. At intervals of 10 min, a 3 mL sample was taken from the solution, filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and subjected to analysis at 664 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer to monitor the degradation of MB. The concentration of the MB solution after adsorption equilibrium was regarded as the initial concentration (C0), and the degradation efficiency (η) was calculated as follows (Jia et al., 2019),
(2)
η =
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100 % =
A 0
-
A t
A 0
× 100 %
where η (%) represents the degradation efficiency, C0 (mg/L) and Ct (mg/L) denote the concentrations of the MB solution at the initial moment and time t, respectively, while A0 and At are the absorbances of the MB solution at the initial moment and time t.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB followed a similar procedure to that of MB, with the exception that the solution pH was adjusted to 4 using HCl. Every 20 mins, a 3 mL sample of the solution was taken, filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and tested at 554 nm using an UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-1, CQDs-2, and CQDs-3 exhibit light brown, dark brown, and yellow colors, respectively. Based on the mass of carbohydrate-rich residue (1.325 g), the product yields of CQDs-1, CQDs-2, and CQDs-3 are 1.0 wt%, 1.5 wt%, and 6.6 wt%, respectively. Notably, CQDs-3 is synthesized from the solid carbon product obtained during the preparation of CQDs-2. The cumulative yield of CQDs from carbohydrate-rich residue can reach 8.1 wt%, and this value surpasses most reports CQDs from lignocellulosic biomass (Zhang et al., 2018).

Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of the three CQDs. UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 reveal prominent absorption peaks at 255 nm and 278 nm, respectively, corresponding to the π-π * transition of the aromatic group sp2 carbon, indicative of a conjugated aromatic structure (Wang et al., 2022). CQDs-3 exhibit extensive UV–vis absorption with a weak peak at 320 nm, attributing to the typical absorption of the aromatic π system or the n-π* transition of the carbonyl (Atchudan et al., 2022). The PL spectrum of CQDs-1 shows an optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 440 nm, Both CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 exhibit optimal excitation wavelengths of 360 nm, with maximum emission wavelengths of 427 nm and 442 nm, respectively. Consistent with most CQDs derived from biomass, the PL spectra of the three CQDs display excitation-dependence characteristics(Atchudan et al., 2020). As the excitation wavelength increases, the maximum fluorescence emission peak undergoes a continuous redshift, attributed to differences in size distributions and surface functional groups among the three CQDs (Atchudan et al., 2018).

The images of the three CQDs solution are shown in Fig. 1 (d). When exposed to sunlight, the aqueous solutions of CQDs appear light brown. While under 360 nm ultraviolet irradiation, the aqueous solutions of CQDs-1, CQDs-2, and CQDs-3 emit light blue, dark green, and green fluorescence, respectively. The QY of CQDs-2 (18.7 %) and CQDs-3 (19.3 %) is nearly double that of CQDs-1 (9.8 %), confirming that nitrogen doping can enhance the relative quantum yields of CQDs.

The stability of CQDs was also studied by comparing their emission intensity at 360 nm after 237 days (see supplementary material). The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-1 and CQDs-3 decreased over this period, while CQDs-2 demonstrated good stability (Krishnaiah et al., 2022). The possible reason for the decrease in emission intensity is that the CQDs aqueous solution was stored in glass bottles for 237 days. The results indicate that nitrogen doping enhances the stability of CQDs, which is beneficial for their practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characteristics of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size distribution of the three CQDs are depicted in Fig. 2 . The particles exhibited excellent dispersion and uniform distribution. The average particle sizes of CQDs-1, CQDs-2, and CQDs-3 were measured as 2.78 ± 0.11 nm, 2.50 ± 0.06 nm, and 2.56 ± 0.14 nm, respectively, indicating their zero-dimensional quasi-spherical nature. HR-TEM images (inset) revealed the presence of (100) planes of graphitic carbon with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm. Remarkably, HR-TEM images of the carbon nuclei of CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 exhibited well-defined hexagonal crystal faces closely resembling graphene structures (Yang et al., 2014).

Two prominent characteristic peaks, D and G, are observed at 1345–1350 cm−1 and 1561–1577 cm−1, respectively, in the Raman spectra of these CQDs (see supplementary material). Peak D corresponds to the out-of-plane vibration of carbon atoms in the disordered structure or vacancy related to the sp3 defect of CQDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups. Peak G is attributed to the in-plane vibration of graphite sp2 carbon in CQDs (Pallavolu et al., 2023). The ID/IG ratios are 1.09 for CQDs-1, 0.90 for CQDs-2, and 0.91 for CQDs-3, indicating enhanced graphitization in nitrogen-doped CQDs, with CQDs-2 exhibiting the highest degree of graphitization.

The wide peak in FTIR spectra of these CQDs at 3000–3700 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H, while absorption peaks at 2955, 1670, 1621, 1406, and 1112 cm−1 represent C-H, C=O, C=C, and C-O stretching vibrations respectively (Chen et al., 2014) (see supplementary material). Peaks at 865 and 605 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of C-H groups. The more pronounced peak at 1043 cm−1 for CQDs-2 indicates the stretching vibration of C-N, confirming successful nitrogen doping during the hydrothermal preparation process (Alossaimi et al., 2023). The presence of abundant hydrophilic functional groups contributes to the good water solubility of CQDs (Lu et al., 2021).

All three CQDs exhibit a wide diffraction peak around 26° in the XRD patterns, attributed to the graphite (002) crystal surface, suggesting a layered structure (Atchudan et al., 2019) (see supplementary material). A weak diffraction peak at 2θ = 46° corresponds to the graphite (100) crystal face, in agreement with the graphite lattice observed in HRTEM images (Jing et al., 2019). The presence of these two peaks indicates that the CQDs possess local graphitized amorphous structures (Wu et al., 2017).

The composition of O, N, and C obtained from XPS survey spectra is summarized in Table 1 . Notably, CQDs-2 exhibit the highest content of the N element, indicative of successful nitrogen doping (see supplementary material). The elevated N/C ratio can enhance the fluorescence performance of CQDs. The C1s bands of CQDs-1 could be split into three peaks at 284.8, 286.5, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, and C=O bonds, respectively. In contrast, the C 1 s bands of CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 can be fitted into four peaks at 284.8, 286.3, 287.8, and 288.8 eV, representing C-C/C=C, C-N, C-O, and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively. The O1s bands can be deconvoluted into 533.3 and 532.0 eV, respectively, corresponding to C-O and C=O bonds. Regarding nitrogen species, the N 1 s spectrum of CQDs-1 displays a peak at 399.6 eV, attributed to pyrrole nitrogen. Conversely, the N 1 s of CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 reveal distinct features, deconvoluted into C-N and N-H at 401.8 and 400.0 eV, indicating the presence of pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen, respectively (Jing et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019). The highest pyridine nitrogen content was 8.43 % for CQDs-2, 1.11 % for CQDs-1, and 3.1 % for CQDs-3. The high N/C ratios in CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 enhance their fluorescence properties.

The aforementioned physicochemical analyses underscore that the addition of urea in the preparation of CQDs effectively facilitates nitrogen doping, leading to the induction of various structural defects. Characterization results further unveil that the presence of nitrogen enhances the graphitization degree of CQDs. The synthesized CQDs exhibit a natural conjugated structure with abundant aliphatic and aromatic hydroxyl groups, carboxylic acid groups, and amino groups on their surface (Fernando et al., 2015). The lower energy required for electron transitions from the π orbital of C=N to the n orbital facilitates the creation of a new radiative recombination path(Fang et al., 2024). Notably, these structural features, particularly the surface-active amino group, contribute to the higher quantum yield observed in CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 compared to CQDs-1, rendering them suitable for luminescent emission (Wang et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis path of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the preceding analysis, the formation mechanism of CQDs is elucidated. Via the lignin-first strategy, the majority of native lignin in birch undergoes depolymerization into phenolic compounds, leaving behind predominantly cellulose and hemicellulose solids. The carbohydrate retention rate of the remaining carbohydrates post-depolymerization is determined to be 76 %, as indicated previously (Jia et al., 2023). The carbohydrate primarily consists of glucan and xylan, alongside constituents such as mannan, galactose, arabinose. During the hydrolysate preparation, catalyzed by H+, the glucoside bond and hydrogen bond in cellulose are broken, yielding oligosaccharide intermediates like L-glucose. In contrast, the hemicellulose component tends to undergo easier breakdown into monosaccharides during depolymerization, subsequently dehydrating to generate furan compounds (furfural or 5-hydroxymethylfurfural). These compounds undergo further decomposition into complex mixture of alcohols, ketones, aldehydes, acids, ethers, esters, furans, and phenols.

The diverse organic compounds within the hydrolysate, such as the large number of short-chain alcohols and ketones, undergo sequential dehydration and condensation in the reactor, influenced by temperature and pressure. Consequently, polymerization and condensation reactions ensue. As the reaction progresses, various small carbon-containing molecules undergo continuous aromatization and carbonization, ultimately leading to nucleation and the formation of CQDs. The surface composition is characterized by abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (Chai et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of pollutants over carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
CQDs with diverse structural properties exhibit varying electrochemical characteristics. Typically, the presence of nitrogen in the amorphous structure of carbon dots facilitates the photogeneration of reaction electrons, thereby enhancing photoreduction efficiency (Emanuele et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). In this study, the photocatalytic performance of three CQDs was evaluated in the degradation of MB under visible light irradiation for 60 min, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Upon addition of CQDs-1, reaching a degradation rate of 27 % after 60 min. Meanwhile, the degradation efficiencies of CQDs-1 and CQDs-3 reached 37 %. According to the Langmuir-Hinshelwood dynamic model (Zhou et al., 2021), in dilute degradation substrate solutions, the photocatalytic kinetic model can be expressed as (Si et al., 2021),
(3)
- ln (
C
C 0
) = k t
where k is the kinetic reaction rate (min−1), C is the concentration of MB at time t, and C is the initial concentration of MB. As depicted in Fig. 3 (b), the photocatalytic degradation reaction conforms to the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation. The kinetic degradation rate of CQDs-2 was measured at 0.00725 min−1, which is 1.4 times higher than that of CQDs-1 (0.00531 min−1), and 1.01 times higher than that of CQDs-3 (0.00717 min−1). CQDs-2 and CQDs-3 exhibited identical kinetic degradation rates.

RhB was subjected to photocatalysis under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 , the degradation ability of RhB in the blank control group without catalyst was negligible, affirming that RhB does not undergo self-degradation solely under visible light irradiation. When CQDs-3 is added, the degradation efficiency is the highest. Upon addition of CQDs-3, the highest degradation efficiency was observed. Under visible light irradiation, the degradation rate reached 24 % within 120 min, whereas the degradation efficiencies of CQDs-1 and CQDs-2 also reached 11 % and 14 %, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation reaction adheres to the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation. The kinetic degradation reaction rate of CQDs-3 was determined to be 0.00218 min−1, which is 2.3 times higher than that of CQDs-1 (0.000934 min−1) and 1.9 times higher than that of CQDs-2 (0.00111 min−1). Notably, CQDs-3 exhibited the highest kinetic degradation reaction rate.

Compared to some previous reports where CQDs exhibited minimal photocatalytic activity (Yu et al., 2014), the CQDs in this study demonstrate relatively robust photocatalytic ability, surpassing that of CQDs prepared from naturally available pomelo (Ramar et al., 2018). The synthesized CQDs are comparable to insoluble graphene nanosheets (Singh et al., 2017), although their degradation efficiency is lower than that of boron-doped CQDs (Peng et al., 2020) and graphene quantum dots (Umrao et al., 2015). Notably, the precursor used in this work, the hydrolysate of depolymerizing birch lignin, is economically more feasible compared to chemical reagents such as citric acid or graphene. Additionally, unlike some reports that require the presence of semiconductors to achieve enhanced photocatalytic degradation, the CQDs prepared in this study exhibit superior photocatalytic performance on their own (Lu et al., 2015). The excellent degree of graphitization and high pyridine nitrogen content contribute significantly to improving the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Although the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs is lower than that of some semiconductor quantum dots (SQCDs), the superior environmentally friendliness, solubility, and biocompatibility of CQDs afford it great potential in applications such as nanoprobes, catalysis, ion sensors, bioimaging, and optoelectronic devices (Liu et al., 2020; Wareing et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Work on doping hetero-atoms like boron to further improve the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs is in ongoing (Miao et al., 2020).

The mechanism underlying the photocatalytic reduction degradation of dye by the CQDs catalyst is deduced. Upon exposure to visible light, CQDs in the solvents undergo excitation, generating electron-hole pairs in the conduction and valence bands. Photogenerated electrons migrate from the valence band to the conduction band of the CQDs, leaving behind holes in the valence band. Due to their higher relative quantum yield, good optical properties, and excellent physical structure, the CQDs suppress the charge recombination rate. This enhanced photon utilization leads to accelerated charge transport efficiency (Fang et al., 2016). Consequently, O can be reduced to ⋅O2 – owing to the presence of photogenerated electrons in the conduction band, and ⋅O2 – can be further react to generate ·OH (Wang et al., 2023). The ⋅O2 – on the conduction band of CQDs effectively degrades the dye due to these free radical molecules being highly reactive species, the positive holes in the valence band directly oxidize dye molecules for degradation. They react with water molecules to produce ·OH radicals, which further enhance the degradation process. Therefore, the CQDs exhibit excellent photocatalytic performance.

Three CQDs were successfully synthesized from carbohydrate-rich residue hydrolysates. All three CQDs exhibited good water solubility and fluorescence properties. Notably, the nitrogen-doped CQDs-2 achieved a quantum yield of 18.7 % and superior graphitization compared to CQDs-1. The total product yield of nitrogen-doped CQDs reached 8.1 wt%. Photocatalytic tests revealed the CQDs’ outstanding efficiency, achieving 37 % degradation of MB, with CQDs-2 showing a high degradation rate of 0.00725 min−1. This study demonstrates a novel approach to CQD synthesis from carbohydrate-rich residue via lignin-first strategy, presenting a new direction for the high-value utilization of all lignocellulosic components.

Ziyu Jia: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Jun Hu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ping Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yunjun Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 52376197) and College Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant 23KJB470024).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2024.131206.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
